question: A manufacturing company has developed a machine learning model to automate the process of identifying and classifying different types of materials — such as metals, plastics, and fabrics — in images captured from its production line. The company needs to evaluate the model's performance using a method that provides detailed insight into accuracy and classification errors. Which option is most suitable for assessing the performance of the classification model?

options.A: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)
options.B: Correlation matrix
options.C: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
options.D: Confusion matrix

rationale: A confusion matrix is the standard tool for evaluating a classification model. It breaks predictions into true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives for each class, providing exactly the detailed insight into classification errors the company needs, and lets you derive precision, recall, and F1. MAE and RMSE are regression metrics for continuous numeric predictions, not class labels. A correlation matrix measures linear relationships between variables and is used in exploratory analysis, not for scoring a classifier.

question: A retail company is exploring machine learning to enhance its customer segmentation and product recommendation systems. The data science team wants a solution that allows them to quickly get started with pre-built models and workflows. Which of the following represent the key features of Amazon SageMaker JumpStart? (Select two)

options.A: Your inference and training data will be used to train the base model
options.B: Pre-trained models are fully customizable for your use case with your data
options.C: You can evaluate, compare, and select Foundation Models quickly based on pre-defined quality and responsibility metrics
options.D: You can build highly accurate ML models using a visual interface without any code
options.E: SageMaker JumpStart provides only public models; proprietary models are not supported

rationale: SageMaker JumpStart is a model hub that lets you deploy pre-trained models and then customize/fine-tune them on your own data (B), and it provides a curated catalog where you can evaluate and compare foundation models against quality and responsibility benchmarks before choosing one (C). Your data is never used to train the underlying base model, so the first option is false. The no-code visual interface belongs to SageMaker Canvas, not JumpStart. JumpStart includes both public and proprietary models, so the last option is also false.

question: A marketing company is researching generative AI technologies to understand how they work and what makes them suitable for automating creative tasks. Which of the following best describes generative AI?

options.A: AI systems limited to performing predefined tasks without adapting to new data or contexts
options.B: Models and algorithms capable of creating new content such as text, images, and audio based on patterns learned from existing data
options.C: A subset of AI that focuses exclusively on improving data retrieval efficiency
options.D: Algorithms that analyze existing data to generate new insights without creating new content

rationale: Generative AI refers to models that learn statistical patterns from large training datasets and then generate genuinely new content — text, images, audio, code. The first option describes rule-based/narrow AI. The third describes search/retrieval systems. The fourth describes traditional analytics that produce insights but not new artifacts. Only the second option captures the defining capability of generative models: producing novel outputs.

question: In generative AI, there is a specific concept used to represent words, sub-words, or characters that the model processes as discrete units of text. What is this concept called?

options.A: Context window
options.B: Vectors
options.C: Tokens
options.D: Embeddings

rationale: Tokens are the discrete units (words, sub-words, or characters) into which text is broken before a model processes it; pricing and context limits for LLMs are measured in tokens. The context window is the maximum number of tokens a model can consider at once. Embeddings are dense numeric vectors that capture semantic meaning, and 'vectors' is the general mathematical form embeddings take. The question asks about the unit of text, which is the token.

question: A technology company wants its AWS environment optimized for governance, cost savings, performance, security, and fault tolerance, using a tool that provides recommendations and best practices. Which AWS tool do you recommend?

options.A: AWS Audit Manager
options.B: AWS Trusted Advisor
options.C: AWS Config
options.D: AWS CloudTrail

rationale: AWS Trusted Advisor continuously inspects your account and gives prioritized best-practice recommendations across cost optimization, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits — matching every category in the scenario. Audit Manager automates evidence collection for compliance audits. AWS Config records and evaluates resource configuration changes. CloudTrail logs API activity for auditing. Only Trusted Advisor delivers proactive, cross-pillar best-practice guidance.

question: A company needs large, high-quality, labeled datasets for training its machine learning models. Which Amazon SageMaker service helps build high-quality training datasets?

options.A: Amazon SageMaker Canvas
options.B: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
options.C: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store
options.D: Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth

rationale: SageMaker Ground Truth is purpose-built for data labeling: it manages human labelers (and automated labeling) to produce high-quality, accurately annotated training datasets. Canvas is a no-code ML modeling tool. JumpStart is a pre-trained model hub. Feature Store is a repository for storing and serving engineered features. Only Ground Truth addresses the creation of labeled datasets.

question: A software development company wants to use Amazon Q Developer for automated code suggestions, task automation, and content generation. Which AWS service powers Amazon Q Developer's core functionalities?

options.A: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
options.B: Amazon Kendra
options.C: Amazon Bedrock
options.D: Amazon Q Apps

rationale: Amazon Q Developer is built on Amazon Bedrock, which provides the foundation models behind its code suggestions and generative capabilities. Kendra is an intelligent enterprise search service. JumpStart is a model hub for SageMaker. Amazon Q Apps is a feature for building lightweight apps within Amazon Q Business — none of these is the underlying engine. Bedrock is the managed foundation-model platform that powers Q.

question: A retail marketing department wants to exclude competitive brand names or sensitive topics from content produced via generative AI prompts. What type of prompting technique does this represent?

options.A: Negative prompting
options.B: Few-shot prompting
options.C: Zero-shot prompting
options.D: Chain-of-thought prompting

rationale: Negative prompting tells the model what to avoid or exclude from its output — here, competitor names and sensitive topics. Few-shot prompting supplies examples to guide format/behavior. Zero-shot prompting gives an instruction with no examples. Chain-of-thought prompting asks the model to reason step by step. Only negative prompting is defined by specifying undesired content.

question: An e-commerce company is considering Amazon Personalize to create tailored experiences based on user behavior such as purchase history and browsing patterns. Which statement best describes Amazon Personalize?

options.A: Elevate the customer experience with ML-powered personalization
options.B: Deploy high-quality, natural-sounding human voices in dozens of languages
options.C: Derive and understand valuable insights from text within documents
options.D: Automatically convert speech to text and gain insights

rationale: Amazon Personalize delivers real-time, ML-powered personalization and recommendations based on user interaction data. The second description is Amazon Polly (text-to-speech). The third is Amazon Comprehend (NLP insights from text). The fourth is Amazon Transcribe (speech-to-text). Only the first matches Personalize's purpose.

question: A retail company needs to perform sentiment analysis for its customer service audio calls. Which AWS services would you recommend?

options.A: Amazon Rekognition and Amazon Transcribe
options.B: Amazon Transcribe and Amazon Translate
options.C: Amazon Transcribe and Amazon Comprehend
options.D: Amazon Translate and Amazon Comprehend

rationale: The calls are audio, so you first use Amazon Transcribe to convert speech to text, then Amazon Comprehend to run sentiment analysis on that text. Rekognition analyzes images/video, not audio sentiment. Translate converts text between languages, which is unnecessary here. The correct pipeline is Transcribe (speech-to-text) followed by Comprehend (sentiment).

question: A healthcare analytics company wants to fine-tune a foundation model in Amazon Bedrock using its own labeled clinical dataset. After fine-tuning, which approach is required to use the customized model?

options.A: Leverage the Amazon Bedrock playground to facilitate fine-tuning
options.B: Leverage batch inference to facilitate fine-tuning
options.C: Use Provisioned Throughput mode, which reserves a specific amount of capacity in advance
options.D: Use On-Demand mode, which charges only for resources consumed

rationale: In Amazon Bedrock, a custom (fine-tuned) model can only be invoked through Provisioned Throughput, where you purchase and reserve dedicated model units. On-Demand pricing is available for base models but not for custom models. The playground is an interactive console for experimentation, not a fine-tuning or serving mechanism. Batch inference is an inference mode, not a way to fine-tune. To run the fine-tuned model you must provision throughput.

question: A company is building a knowledge management system with Amazon Bedrock and needs to store and retrieve embeddings. Which is the default vector database supported by Knowledge Bases for Amazon Bedrock?

options.A: OpenSearch Serverless vector store
options.B: MongoDB
options.C: Redis Enterprise Cloud
options.D: Amazon Aurora

rationale: When you create a Bedrock Knowledge Base without specifying an existing vector store, Bedrock provisions an Amazon OpenSearch Serverless vector collection as the default. Bedrock can also integrate with other vector stores (Aurora pgvector, Pinecone, Redis Enterprise, MongoDB Atlas), but those must be configured explicitly. The out-of-the-box default is OpenSearch Serverless.

question: A media company's AI image-generation model consistently produces biased outputs because the training data is imbalanced and underrepresents certain demographic groups. What is the most suitable strategy to mitigate this bias?

options.A: Apply model regularization techniques to address the imbalance
options.B: Augment the data by generating new instances of data for underrepresented groups
options.C: Use another model that can handle the imbalance
options.D: Leverage human intervention to manually correct the imbalanced dataset

rationale: The root cause is an imbalanced dataset, so the most effective fix is data augmentation — generating additional examples for underrepresented groups so the model sees balanced representation during training. Regularization combats overfitting, not representation imbalance. Swapping models does not address biased input data. Manual correction does not scale. Balancing the data directly targets the source of the bias.

question: A company is unsure how frequently it will use Amazon Bedrock and wants a flexible pricing model to experiment without long-term commitment. Which pricing model is most appropriate?

options.A: Reserved instances, which offer lower rates for a long-term commitment
options.B: Provisioned Throughput, which reserves capacity in advance for a discounted rate
options.C: On-Demand pricing, which charges only for actual usage with no long-term commitment
options.D: Spot instances, which provide savings but risk interruptions

rationale: On-Demand pricing in Amazon Bedrock charges per input/output token with no commitment, which is ideal for unpredictable, experimental workloads. Provisioned Throughput requires committing to reserved model units, better suited to steady high-volume production. Reserved and Spot instances are EC2 concepts, not Bedrock pricing options. On-Demand maximizes flexibility for uncertain usage.

question: A customer support company uses few-shot prompting with an Amazon Bedrock LLM so the chatbot can correctly identify user intent. What data should the few-shot examples contain?

options.A: Model responses paired with the correct user intent
options.B: User input paired with the correct user intent, providing examples of queries and their corresponding intent
options.C: User input paired with the correct model response
options.D: User input paired with the model response (generated output paired with the input)

rationale: For intent classification, each few-shot example should pair a representative user input with the correct intent label, so the model learns to map new queries to the right intent. Pairing model responses or model outputs with intents teaches the wrong mapping. The goal is input → intent, so examples must demonstrate exactly that relationship.

question: Which of the following embedding models is most suitable for differentiating the contextual meanings of words when applied to different phrases?

options.A: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
options.B: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
options.C: Word2Vec
options.D: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT)

rationale: BERT produces contextual embeddings: the same word receives different vector representations depending on the surrounding sentence, capturing contextual meaning (e.g., 'bank' of a river vs. a financial bank). Word2Vec generates a single static vector per word regardless of context. PCA and SVD are dimensionality-reduction techniques, not language embedding models. BERT is the right choice for context-sensitive meaning.

question: Which explanation best describes the difference between Shapley values and Partial Dependence Plots (PDP) for model explainability?

options.A: Shapley values give a local explanation by quantifying each feature's contribution to a specific prediction, while PDP gives a global explanation showing the marginal effect of a feature across the dataset
options.B: Shapley values are visual while PDP is numeric
options.C: Shapley values are global while PDP is local
options.D: Both are global methods, but Shapley values are computationally cheaper than PDP

rationale: Shapley values explain an individual prediction by attributing how much each feature contributed for that specific instance — a local explanation. PDPs show the average marginal effect of a feature on predictions across the whole dataset — a global explanation. Shapley values are actually more computationally expensive, not cheaper, making the other options incorrect.

question: A company wants multiple ML models across departments to leverage knowledge from one another to optimize accuracy on related tasks. Which approach is most suitable for this cross-model optimization?

options.A: Incremental training, updating models with new data without full retraining
options.B: Reinforcement learning, where an agent learns via rewards and penalties
options.C: Transfer learning, where a model pre-trained on one task is adapted to a different but related task
options.D: Self-supervised learning, where the model learns from unlabeled data

rationale: Transfer learning lets you take knowledge a model has already learned and reuse it to improve a different but related task — exactly 'models learning from each other' across departments. Incremental training just keeps updating one model with fresh data. Reinforcement learning is about reward-driven decision making. Self-supervised learning generates labels from unlabeled data. Transfer learning is the technique for reusing learned representations across related tasks.

question: A healthcare ML model has high accuracy on training data but drops significantly on new, unseen patient records in production. What is the most effective approach to improve generalization?

options.A: Reduce the amount of training data to eliminate noise
options.B: Increase the amount of training data so the model learns more diverse patterns and generalizes better
options.C: Use hyperparameter tuning such as regularization, learning rate, and dropout
options.D: Swap the model with a state-of-the-art generative AI model

rationale: The symptom — excellent on training data, poor on new data — is overfitting. Increasing the volume and diversity of training data is one of the most effective remedies because it exposes the model to a wider range of real-world examples and reduces memorization. Reducing data would worsen overfitting. Hyperparameter tuning (regularization, dropout) also helps, but adding more representative data is the most directly effective fix. Swapping to a generative model does not address the data-coverage problem.

question: Which of the following are correct statements regarding the AWS Global Infrastructure? (Select two)

options.A: Each AWS Region consists of a minimum of two Availability Zones
options.B: Each AWS Region consists of a minimum of three Availability Zones
options.C: Each AWS Region consists of two or more Edge Locations
options.D: Each Availability Zone consists of one or more discrete data centers
options.E: Each Availability Zone consists of two or more discrete data centers

rationale: An AWS Region contains a minimum of three isolated, physically separate Availability Zones, and each Availability Zone is made up of one or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. Edge Locations are part of the CloudFront CDN and are not how Regions are defined. Specifying 'two AZs' or 'two or more data centers per AZ' does not match AWS's published definitions.

question: What best summarizes the difference between model inference and model evaluation in generative AI?

options.A: Model evaluation compares model outputs to choose the best model for a use case, whereas model inference is a model generating an output from a given input
options.B: Model inference compares outputs to choose the best model, whereas model evaluation is generating an output from an input
options.C: Both refer to comparing and selecting the best model for a use case
options.D: Both refer to a model generating an output from an input

rationale: Model evaluation is the process of assessing and comparing models using metrics or human review to decide which is best for a use case. Model inference is the runtime act of feeding a prompt to a model and getting a generated response. The first option states both correctly; the others swap the definitions or collapse the two distinct concepts together.

question: A software company is comparing Amazon Q and Amazon Bedrock. Which statements correctly describe them? (Select two)

options.A: With Amazon Q you can choose the underlying foundation model, but Amazon Bedrock does not allow it
options.B: Both Amazon Q and Amazon Bedrock are generative AI assistants that let you create pre-packaged applications
options.C: Amazon Bedrock is a generative AI assistant for pre-packaged applications, whereas Amazon Q provides an environment to build and scale applications using a foundation model
options.D: Amazon Q is a generative AI-powered assistant for pre-packaged applications, whereas Amazon Bedrock provides an environment to build and scale generative AI applications using a foundation model
options.E: With Amazon Bedrock you can choose the underlying foundation model, but Amazon Q does not allow you to choose it

rationale: Amazon Q is a ready-to-use generative AI assistant for pre-packaged business/developer experiences, while Amazon Bedrock is a developer platform for building and scaling custom generative AI apps on a choice of foundation models (D). Bedrock lets you select among many foundation models, whereas Amazon Q abstracts the model away and does not expose model choice (E). The other options reverse these roles or wrongly claim Q lets you pick the model.

question: A retail company has product catalog PDFs and wants its Amazon Bedrock LLM chatbot to give current, relevant answers. Which approach is most cost-effective?

options.A: Attach the contents of all product catalog PDFs to each customer query
options.B: Attach the contents of a single product catalog PDF to each customer query
options.C: Fine-tune the LLM with data extracted from the product catalog PDFs
options.D: Use a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system by indexing all product catalog PDFs and having the chatbot reference it

rationale: RAG indexes the catalog once and retrieves only the most relevant passages per query, keeping prompts small and letting you update content by re-indexing rather than retraining — the most cost-effective and current approach. Stuffing entire PDFs into every prompt explodes token costs and may exceed the context window. Fine-tuning is expensive, must be redone whenever the catalog changes, and is poorly suited to frequently changing factual data.

question: Which of the following are CORRECT statements regarding Amazon ML/AI services? (Select two)

options.A: Amazon Comprehend uses machine learning to convert speech to text
options.B: Amazon Transcribe builds conversational interfaces using voice and text
options.C: Amazon Rekognition extracts key phrases and organizes text files by topic
options.D: Amazon Comprehend uses machine learning to find insights and relationships in text
options.E: Amazon Polly deploys high-quality, natural-sounding human voices in dozens of languages

rationale: Amazon Comprehend is an NLP service that finds insights and relationships in text — entities, key phrases, sentiment, topics (D). Amazon Polly is a text-to-speech service that produces lifelike voices in many languages (E). Speech-to-text is Transcribe (not Comprehend). Conversational interfaces are Amazon Lex (not Transcribe). Key-phrase extraction/topic modeling is Comprehend (not Rekognition, which analyzes images and video).

question: An e-commerce company wants to analyze thousands of daily customer reviews for sentiment. Which would you recommend? (Select two)

options.A: Amazon Textract
options.B: Amazon Bedrock
options.C: Amazon Rekognition
options.D: Amazon Personalize
options.E: Amazon Comprehend

rationale: Amazon Comprehend provides built-in sentiment analysis classifying text as positive, negative, neutral, or mixed — the most direct fit. Amazon Bedrock foundation models can also perform sentiment analysis through prompting, making it a valid second choice. Textract extracts text from scanned documents. Rekognition analyzes images/video. Personalize generates recommendations — none of these performs sentiment analysis.

question: A media company's marketing team wants to use Amazon Bedrock to produce more creative scripts for an ad campaign. What do you recommend?

options.A: Use higher Top-P to get more creative responses
options.B: Use higher Temperature to get more creative responses
options.C: Use lower Temperature to get more creative responses
options.D: Use lower Top-P to get more creative responses

rationale: Temperature controls randomness: a higher temperature flattens the probability distribution so the model is more likely to pick less-probable tokens, producing more varied and creative output. Lower temperature makes output more deterministic. While raising Top-P also increases diversity, the conventional and most direct lever for 'more creative' output is increasing temperature; lowering either parameter reduces creativity.

question: A healthcare company wants to understand a primary advantage of using generative AI in the AWS cloud. What is one of those advantages?

options.A: Generative AI can automate the creation of new data based on existing patterns, enhancing productivity and innovation
options.B: Generative AI ensures 100% security against all cyber threats
options.C: Generative AI can replace all human roles in software development
options.D: Generative AI can perform all cloud maintenance tasks without human intervention

rationale: A genuine benefit of generative AI is automating the creation of new content/data from learned patterns, boosting productivity and innovation. The other options are absolute, unrealistic claims — no technology guarantees 100% security, replaces all human roles, or eliminates all human oversight. Responsible messaging avoids such overstatements.

question: A manufacturing company wants an Amazon Bedrock generative AI application that automates monitoring of inventory, sales, and supply-chain data and recommends optimal reorder points. What do you recommend?

options.A: Agents for Amazon Bedrock
options.B: Watermark detection for Amazon Bedrock
options.C: Knowledge Bases for Amazon Bedrock
options.D: Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock

rationale: Agents for Amazon Bedrock orchestrate multi-step tasks: they can call APIs/Lambda functions, query data sources, and take actions such as calculating and recommending reorder quantities — exactly the action-oriented automation described. Knowledge Bases add retrieval (RAG) but do not execute actions. Guardrails enforce safety/content policies. Watermark detection identifies AI-generated images. Only Agents perform the automated, action-taking workflow.

question: A financial services company needs to monitor and track the performance and usage of multiple SageMaker models hosted on endpoints for real-time inference. Which SageMaker capability do you recommend?

options.A: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
options.B: Amazon SageMaker Clarify
options.C: Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth
options.D: Amazon SageMaker Model Dashboard

rationale: The SageMaker Model Dashboard gives a unified, central view of all deployed models, their endpoints, monitoring status, and any detected issues. JumpStart is a model hub. Clarify handles bias detection and explainability. Ground Truth handles data labeling. The Model Dashboard is the monitoring and management surface.

question: A legal research firm wants Amazon Bedrock to deploy a fully managed end-to-end Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) workflow over its legal documents. What do you recommend?

options.A: Knowledge Bases for Amazon Bedrock
options.B: Continued pre-training in Amazon Bedrock
options.C: Watermark detection for Amazon Bedrock
options.D: Guardrails for Amazon Bedrock

rationale: Knowledge Bases for Amazon Bedrock is the fully managed RAG capability: it handles ingestion, chunking, embedding, vector storage, retrieval, and prompt augmentation end to end. Continued pre-training adapts a model on unlabeled data but is not RAG. Guardrails enforce safety policies. Watermark detection identifies AI-generated images. Knowledge Bases is the managed RAG solution.

question: A company needs to understand how Transformer models work for NLP tasks like translation and summarization. Which best summarizes how Transformer models work?

options.A: Transformer models use a self-attention mechanism and contextual embeddings
options.B: Transformer models train two neural networks in a competitive manner
options.C: Transformer models create new data by iteratively making controlled random changes to a sample
options.D: Transformer models learn a compact representation of data called latent space

rationale: Transformers rely on a self-attention mechanism that weighs the relationship of every token to every other token, producing rich contextual embeddings and enabling parallel processing of sequences. Training two networks competitively describes GANs. Iteratively adding/removing controlled noise describes diffusion models. Learning a compact latent representation describes autoencoders/VAEs. Self-attention with contextual embeddings is the hallmark of the Transformer.

question: An admissions committee wants to detect when applicants use generative AI to write essays so that submissions reflect the applicants' own work. What specific issue is the committee primarily trying to address?

options.A: Misinterpretation
options.B: Hallucination
options.C: Bias
options.D: Plagiarism

rationale: Submitting AI-generated text as one's own original work is a form of plagiarism — passing off content the applicant did not author. Hallucination refers to a model fabricating false information. Bias refers to systematically skewed outputs. Misinterpretation is a general comprehension error. The integrity concern here is authorship/originality, which is plagiarism.

question: A retail analytics team is calculating statistical measures and using visualizations to uncover patterns before model development. Which phase of the data science process is this?

options.A: Model Evaluation
options.B: Data Preparation
options.C: Data Augmentation
options.D: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

rationale: Computing summary statistics and visualizing data to discover patterns, distributions, and relationships before modeling is the definition of Exploratory Data Analysis. Data Preparation focuses on cleaning and transforming data. Data Augmentation creates synthetic additional samples. Model Evaluation assesses a trained model's performance. The described activities — summarizing and visualizing to understand structure — are EDA.

question: A developer building a customer-churn application is collaborating with a research team to select the best model. What should the developer ask the research team to do to ensure the best model is selected?

options.A: Determine the cost constraints for model training
options.B: Identify potential data sources for the application
options.C: Define the use case of the application narrowly
options.D: Define the target audience of the application broadly

rationale: AWS guidance for choosing the right model emphasizes defining the use case narrowly and precisely. A tightly scoped problem definition lets the team select and evaluate models against a clear, measurable objective. Broad audiences, cost-only criteria, or data sourcing are useful inputs but do not by themselves drive correct model selection the way a precise use-case definition does.

question: A company has deployed models on Amazon Bedrock and needs detailed visibility into all model invocations (input data and output responses) for usage tracking, auditing, and troubleshooting. Which solution is most suitable?

options.A: Enable model invocation logging for detailed logging of all requests and responses
options.B: Analyze Amazon Bedrock model invocation events in Amazon EventBridge
options.C: Use AWS Config to monitor configuration changes initiated by Bedrock
options.D: Use AWS CloudTrail logs to track Amazon Bedrock API calls

rationale: Bedrock's model invocation logging captures the full request and response payloads (prompts and completions) and writes them to Amazon S3 and/or CloudWatch Logs — exactly the input/output visibility needed for auditing and troubleshooting. CloudTrail records that an API call occurred and by whom, but not the prompt/response content. EventBridge handles event routing. Config tracks resource configuration, not invocation content.

question: A financial services company's deployed fraud/credit-risk models are not accurate enough. Which approach would you recommend to enhance accuracy?

options.A: Reduce the batch size to process fewer samples per training step
options.B: Decrease the learning rate to take smaller optimization steps
options.C: Increase regularization to improve accuracy
options.D: Increase the number of epochs to train the model for more iterations over the dataset

rationale: If a model is underfitting and not yet accurate enough, training for more epochs lets it iterate over the data additional times and continue lowering the loss, often improving accuracy. Increasing regularization typically reduces overfitting by constraining the model, which can lower training accuracy. Batch size and learning-rate tweaks affect training dynamics but are not the primary lever for simply 'more learning.' More epochs is the most direct way to improve an under-trained model.

question: A company using Amazon Bedrock wants to regulate the number of most-likely candidates considered for the next word in the model's output. Which inference parameter do you recommend?

options.A: Top K
options.B: Top P
options.C: Stop sequences
options.D: Temperature

rationale: Top K limits sampling to the K most-probable next tokens — it directly controls the number of candidate tokens considered. Top P (nucleus sampling) selects the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches P, so it controls a probability mass, not a fixed count. Temperature scales the overall randomness. Stop sequences define where generation halts. To cap the count of candidates, use Top K.

question: Which AWS service is specifically designed to provide insights into model predictions by explaining how input features contribute to the final output?

options.A: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor
options.B: Amazon SageMaker Canvas
options.C: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store
options.D: Amazon SageMaker Clarify

rationale: Amazon SageMaker Clarify provides explainability (using feature-attribution methods such as SHAP) to show how each input feature influences a prediction, and it also detects bias. Model Monitor watches deployed models for data/quality drift but does not explain feature contributions. Canvas is no-code modeling. Feature Store manages features. Clarify is the explainability and bias-detection service.

question: A healthcare technology company needs to understand model customization methods in Amazon Bedrock. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Continued pre-training uses labeled data and fine-tuning also uses labeled data
options.B: Continued pre-training uses unlabeled data, whereas fine-tuning uses labeled data
options.C: Continued pre-training uses unlabeled data and fine-tuning also uses unlabeled data
options.D: Continued pre-training uses labeled data, whereas fine-tuning uses unlabeled data

rationale: In Amazon Bedrock, continued pre-training further trains a model on large amounts of unlabeled, domain-specific text to broaden its domain knowledge, while fine-tuning uses smaller labeled prompt-completion datasets to specialize the model for a task. Continued pre-training = unlabeled, fine-tuning = labeled. The other combinations mislabel one or both methods.

question: A retail company processes datasets under 1 GB and does not need immediate responses, using results for weekly strategy meetings. Which SageMaker inference method is most suitable?

options.A: Asynchronous inference
options.B: Batch inference (batch transform)
options.C: Real-time inference
options.D: Serverless inference

rationale: Because the workload is not latency-sensitive and processes whole datasets on a schedule, batch transform (batch inference) is the most cost-effective option: it spins up resources, processes the dataset, writes results, and shuts down. Real-time and serverless inference are for low-latency, per-request responses. Asynchronous inference suits large payloads or long processing times with queued requests. For periodic, delay-tolerant scoring of full datasets, batch transform is the natural fit.

question: A retail company is considering using MLflow with Amazon SageMaker. Which statement best defines this use?

options.A: Label data using human-in-the-loop
options.B: Leverage no-code ML
options.C: Perform automatic model tuning
options.D: Manage machine learning experiments

rationale: MLflow is an open-source platform for managing the ML lifecycle, and its integration with SageMaker is used to track and manage experiments — logging parameters, metrics, and artifacts and comparing runs. Human-in-the-loop labeling is Ground Truth. No-code ML is Canvas. Automatic model tuning is SageMaker's hyperparameter tuning. MLflow's role here is experiment management.

question: A security company is evaluating Amazon Rekognition. Which use case is NOT a good fit for Amazon Rekognition?

options.A: Enable multilingual user experiences in your applications
options.B: Face-based user identity verification
options.C: Celebrity recognition
options.D: Searchable media libraries

rationale: Amazon Rekognition is a computer-vision service for images and video: it supports face verification, celebrity recognition, and building searchable media libraries. Enabling multilingual user experiences (translation/localization) is a language task handled by Amazon Translate, not a vision task — so it is the use case that does not fit Rekognition.

question: A company fine-tuned a Bedrock foundation model on data that includes confidential information and wants to ensure the model's responses do not leak it. What is the most efficient approach?

options.A: Use encryption to protect confidential information in model responses
options.B: Mask the confidential information in responses using Amazon Bedrock Guardrails
options.C: Swap Bedrock for SageMaker and rebuild with built-in algorithms
options.D: Delete the model, remove confidential data from training data, and fine-tune again

rationale: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails can detect and mask or block sensitive information (including PII) in model outputs through filters and sensitive-information policies, providing an efficient way to prevent leakage without rebuilding anything. Encryption protects data at rest/in transit but does not stop a model from emitting memorized confidential content. Rebuilding on SageMaker or re-doing fine-tuning is far more costly. Guardrails is the efficient control.

question: A retail company wants pre-trained computer vision models for fast deployment with the flexibility to customize them. What do you suggest?

options.A: Amazon DeepRacer
options.B: Amazon SageMaker
options.C: Amazon Textract
options.D: Amazon Rekognition

rationale: Amazon Rekognition offers ready-to-use, pre-trained computer-vision APIs and also supports Custom Labels, letting the company train customized models for specific objects/scenes with relatively little data. DeepRacer is a reinforcement-learning racing toy. Textract extracts text from documents. SageMaker is a general ML platform that would require building models from scratch. Rekognition provides both out-of-the-box and customizable vision capabilities.

question: Which of the following represents a valid use case for a generative AI-powered model?

options.A: Utilizing generative AI to predict housing prices based on historical market data
options.B: Using generative AI to create photorealistic images from textual descriptions
options.C: Applying generative AI for financial analysis to forecast stock market trends
options.D: Classifying medical images to detect anomalies or diagnose diseases

rationale: Generating photorealistic images from text prompts (text-to-image) is a quintessential generative AI use case — the model creates new content. Predicting housing prices and forecasting stock trends are regression/forecasting tasks best handled by traditional predictive ML. Classifying medical images is a discriminative classification task. Only image generation from text involves creating new content, the defining purpose of generative models.

question: A biotechnology company wants to turn a Bedrock foundation model into a domain expert in genomics. Which two approaches are most effective? (Select two)

options.A: Incremental Learning, to learn new data without forgetting previous information
options.B: Domain Adaptation Fine-Tuning on domain-specific labeled data
options.C: Continued Pre-Training on a large corpus of domain-specific data to learn terminology and context
options.D: Reinforcement Learning through trial and error with rewards
options.E: Supervised Learning on labeled data to predict specific outputs

rationale: To specialize a foundation model in a domain, the two effective techniques are continued pre-training on a large corpus of unlabeled domain text (teaching the model genomics terminology and patterns) and fine-tuning on domain-specific labeled examples (adapting it to target tasks). Generic incremental, reinforcement, and plain supervised learning are not the Bedrock model-customization methods designed for domain expertise; continued pre-training and fine-tuning are.

question: A content marketing team notices their generative AI model can only consider a certain amount of text at once. What is the concept that defines the maximum amount of text the model can process at one time?

options.A: Context window — the amount of text/information the model can consider at once, measured in tokens
options.B: Tokens — units of text the model processes
options.C: Embeddings — numerical representations of text
options.D: Character count — the number of individual characters in a text

rationale: The context window is the maximum number of tokens a model can take in (prompt plus generated output) at one time, which limits the length and complexity of inputs. Tokens are the individual units of text. Embeddings are numeric vector representations of meaning. Character count is a raw character total. The limit on how much text the model can consider at once is precisely the context window.

question: Which generative AI techniques are used in the Amazon Q Business web application workflow? (Select two)

options.A: Generative adversarial network (GAN)
options.B: Variational autoencoders (VAE)
options.C: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
options.D: Large Language Model (LLM)

rationale: Amazon Q Business answers questions by using a large language model (LLM) combined with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) — it retrieves relevant passages from your connected enterprise data and feeds them to the LLM to generate grounded responses. GANs and VAEs are generative architectures used mainly for images and synthetic data, not for Q Business's text retrieval-and-generation workflow.

question: A marketing analytics company wants to improve the quality of AI-generated content by crafting effective prompts. What are the key constituents of a good prompting technique?

options.A: Instructions, Parameters, Input data, Output Indicator
options.B: Instructions, Hyperparameters, Input data, Output Indicator
options.C: Hyperparameters, Context, Input data, Output Indicator
options.D: Instructions, Context, Input data, Output Indicator

rationale: A well-structured prompt generally contains four elements: Instructions (the task), Context (background that guides the response), Input data (the specific content to act on), and an Output Indicator (the desired format/type of answer). Hyperparameters and inference parameters are model settings, not parts of the prompt text. The correct set is Instructions, Context, Input data, Output Indicator.

question: A financial services company needs to understand Foundation Models (FMs). Which statement is correct regarding FMs in generative AI?

options.A: FMs use supervised learning to create labels from input data; fine-tuning an FM is self-supervised
options.B: FMs use self-supervised learning to create labels from input data; fine-tuning an FM is a supervised learning process
options.C: FMs use self-supervised learning; fine-tuning is also self-supervised
options.D: FMs use supervised learning; fine-tuning is also supervised

rationale: Foundation models are pre-trained with self-supervised learning, where the training objective (e.g., predicting masked or next tokens) generates its own labels from massive unlabeled corpora. Fine-tuning then uses a curated labeled dataset (supervised learning) to specialize the model for a task. The correct combination is self-supervised pre-training followed by supervised fine-tuning.

question: In the AWS Shared Responsibility Model, which statement best describes the division of responsibilities between the customer and AWS?

options.A: AWS handles all security including data encryption, access management, and application security; the customer only manages virtual machines
options.B: AWS configures and manages the security settings of the customer's applications; the customer manages the underlying hardware
options.C: Customers ensure physical security of data centers; AWS monitors network traffic and identities
options.D: AWS is responsible for security 'of' the cloud (infrastructure, hardware, software), while the customer is responsible for security 'in' the cloud (data, applications, access management)

rationale: Under the Shared Responsibility Model, AWS secures the cloud itself — the physical infrastructure, hardware, and foundational software — while the customer secures what they put in the cloud: their data, application configuration, identity and access management, and encryption choices. The 'of the cloud' vs 'in the cloud' split is the canonical description.

question: A streaming service must classify thousands of movies into 20 categories and needs clear insight into how each classification decision is made and which features influence it. Which ML algorithm is most suitable?

options.A: Support Vector Machines (SVMs)
options.B: Decision Trees
options.C: Logistic Regression
options.D: Neural Networks

rationale: Decision trees are inherently interpretable: the path of feature splits from root to leaf documents exactly why an item was placed in a category, and they handle multi-class problems well. The requirement stresses transparency and understanding which features drive decisions, which favors a 'white-box' model. SVMs and neural networks are powerful but opaque. Logistic regression, while interpretable, is naturally a linear/binary approach less suited to 20-class rule-traceable categorization.

question: An LLM chatbot generates responses that look plausible and factual but are actually incorrect. What is this phenomenon called?

options.A: Underfitting
options.B: Data drift
options.C: Overfitting
options.D: Hallucination

rationale: When a model confidently produces fluent but false or fabricated information, it is hallucinating. Underfitting is when a model is too simple to capture patterns and performs poorly everywhere. Overfitting is memorizing training data and failing on new data. Data drift is when input distributions change over time and degrade accuracy. The specific term for plausible-sounding but incorrect generated content is hallucination.

question: A company on Amazon Bedrock wants to reduce usage cost while including prompt examples of up to 10 sample tasks in each input. Which approach minimizes cost most effectively?

options.A: Reduce the temperature inference parameter
options.B: Reduce the top-p inference parameter
options.C: Reduce the number of tokens in the input
options.D: Reduce the batch size while training the model

rationale: Amazon Bedrock on-demand pricing is driven primarily by the number of input and output tokens, so the most direct way to cut cost is to reduce input tokens — for example by trimming or compressing the lengthy few-shot examples. Temperature and top-p change output style/randomness, not price. Batch size relates to training, which is not happening at inference time. Fewer tokens means lower cost.

question: A mall's AI security system disproportionately flags individuals from a specific ethnic group, likely because some groups are over/underrepresented in the training data. Which type of bias is most likely responsible?

options.A: Observer bias
options.B: Sampling bias
options.C: Measurement bias
options.D: Confirmation bias

rationale: When certain groups are over- or under-represented in the training data, the resulting skew is sampling bias — the sample does not reflect the real population, so the model performs unevenly across groups. Observer bias comes from subjective labeling. Measurement bias comes from flawed instruments or features. Confirmation bias comes from interpreting results to fit expectations. The described representation imbalance in the data is sampling bias.

question: An e-commerce chatbot on Amazon Bedrock should continuously learn and improve from real-time customer interactions, optimizing responses based on feedback over time. Which approach is most suitable?

options.A: Incremental training to adapt over time without forgetting prior learning
options.B: Supervised learning using datasets of positive and negative interactions
options.C: Transfer learning to apply knowledge from one domain to another
options.D: Reinforcement learning (RL), where rewards are generated from positive customer feedback to optimize responses

rationale: Reinforcement learning fits 'learning from interaction and feedback to maximize a reward': the chatbot can treat positive customer feedback as reward signals and adjust its policy to produce better responses over time — the conceptual basis of techniques like RLHF. Incremental and supervised learning update on new labeled data but do not optimize via a reward signal from ongoing interactions. Transfer learning is about reusing knowledge across domains. RL is the approach driven by feedback/rewards.

question: A tech company building a RAG solution with Amazon Bedrock needs a database for fast index lookups, similarity search, full-text search, and relevance ranking over a large dataset. Which database is most appropriate?

options.A: Amazon Aurora, a managed relational database optimized for transactional workloads
options.B: Amazon DocumentDB (MongoDB compatible), a managed NoSQL document database
options.C: Amazon OpenSearch Service, designed for fast search with full-text search, indexing, and similarity scoring
options.D: Amazon DynamoDB, a managed NoSQL database for low-latency key lookups

rationale: Amazon OpenSearch Service is built for search workloads: it provides full-text search, fast indexing, k-NN vector similarity search, and relevance scoring — precisely the capabilities a RAG retriever needs. Aurora (relational) and DynamoDB (key-value) are transactional databases not optimized for similarity ranking. DocumentDB targets document storage rather than relevance-ranked search. OpenSearch is the right retrieval engine for RAG.

question: A financial services company is exploring ML approaches and needs to know which methods fall under supervised learning. Which of the following are examples of supervised learning? (Select two)

options.A: Linear regression
options.B: Association rule learning
options.C: Document classification
options.D: Clustering
options.E: Neural network (in unsupervised settings)

rationale: Supervised learning trains on labeled data with known outcomes. Linear regression (predicting a continuous target from labeled examples) and document classification (assigning labeled categories to text) are both supervised. Association rule learning and clustering are unsupervised — they find patterns or groupings in unlabeled data. Neural networks can be supervised or unsupervised depending on setup, but the two clear supervised examples are linear regression and document classification.

question: A company wants a unified search solution connecting multiple data repositories, third-party document repositories, and FAQs to create a new search experience for employees. Which ML-powered AWS service offers these search features?

options.A: Amazon Comprehend
options.B: Amazon Textract
options.C: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler
options.D: Amazon Kendra

rationale: Amazon Kendra is an intelligent, ML-powered enterprise search service that connects to many data sources (S3, SharePoint, databases, FAQs, third-party repositories) and returns precise, natural-language answers. Comprehend analyzes text for insights. Textract extracts text from documents. Data Wrangler prepares data for ML. For a unified, intelligent search experience across repositories, Kendra is the purpose-built service.

question: A developer is migrating AI infrastructure to AWS to support training, deploying, and managing LLM-based solutions. Which AWS services would you recommend? (Select two)

options.A: Amazon Bedrock
options.B: Amazon SageMaker JumpStart
options.C: AWS Trainium
options.D: Amazon Q
options.E: AWS Inferentia

rationale: For developing LLM-based solutions, Amazon Bedrock provides managed access to foundation models with customization, RAG, and agents, and Amazon SageMaker JumpStart provides pre-trained LLMs you can deploy and fine-tune — both are services for building LLM applications. Trainium and Inferentia are specialized hardware accelerator chips (for training and inference respectively), not development services. Amazon Q is a ready-made assistant rather than a platform for building custom LLM solutions.

question: A financial services company is experimenting with the Top P inference parameter in Amazon Bedrock. How does Top P influence the model response?

options.A: Specifies the sequences of characters that stop the model from generating further tokens
options.B: Influences the number of most-likely candidates the model considers for the next token
options.C: Influences the percentage of most-likely candidates the model considers for the next token
options.D: Influences the likelihood of selecting lower-probability outputs, impacting creativity

rationale: Top P (nucleus sampling) restricts token selection to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches the threshold P — it is governed by a percentage of the probability mass. Top K fixes the count of candidate tokens. Stop sequences define where generation halts. Temperature is the parameter most associated with overall creativity/randomness. Top P controls the cumulative-probability (percentage) cutoff.

question: A consulting firm is comparing Foundation Models (FMs) and Large Language Models (LLMs). What is a key difference between FMs and LLMs in generative AI?

options.A: FMs are only for academic research while LLMs are for commercial applications
options.B: FMs provide broad, generalized capabilities as a base for many AI applications, whereas LLMs are specialized for understanding and generating human language
options.C: FMs are only for text generation while LLMs generate images, video, and audio
options.D: LLMs are pre-trained and fine-tunable, whereas FMs are not pre-trained and are built from scratch each time

rationale: Foundation models are large models pre-trained on broad data that serve as a versatile base for many downstream tasks and modalities. Large language models are a subset of foundation models specialized for natural language. The key distinction is breadth: FMs are the general base, and LLMs are the language-focused specialization. The other options state factual errors about research-only use, modality limits, or pre-training.

question: A robotics company wants to understand how reinforcement learning works for its autonomous robots. How does reinforcement learning work?

options.A: It transforms raw data into a new feature space to reduce dimensionality
options.B: It uses unsupervised techniques to cluster data points without feedback
options.C: An agent interacts with an environment by taking actions and receiving rewards or penalties, learning a policy to maximize cumulative rewards over time
options.D: It uses supervised algorithms to label data and make predictions

rationale: Reinforcement learning is defined by an agent that takes actions in an environment, receives rewards or penalties as feedback, and gradually learns a policy that maximizes cumulative reward — ideal for sequential decision-making like robot navigation. The other options describe dimensionality reduction, unsupervised clustering, and supervised learning, which are different paradigms.

question: A retail company wants ML for accurate resource/demand planning to reduce stockouts. Which is the best fit for the Amazon Forecast service?

options.A: Detect and categorize toxic audio for a safe online environment
options.B: Recommendations tailored to a user's profile, behavior, preferences, and history
options.C: Predict product demand to accurately manage inventory and pricing across store locations
options.D: Design conversational solutions for FAQs in technical support and HR

rationale: Amazon Forecast is a time-series forecasting service, so predicting future product demand to optimize inventory and pricing is exactly its purpose. Toxic-audio moderation is unrelated. Personalized recommendations are Amazon Personalize. Conversational FAQ bots are Amazon Lex/Q. Demand forecasting is the Forecast use case.

question: A company's app translates English text into other languages and wants to evaluate translation accuracy. Which metric is most appropriate?

options.A: Accuracy, a general metric measuring the proportion of correctly predicted outputs
options.B: BERT score, which uses contextual embeddings to evaluate semantic similarity between machine and reference translations
options.C: BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) score, designed to evaluate machine-translated text by comparing it with one or more reference translations
options.D: ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation), used mainly for summarization quality

rationale: BLEU is the standard metric for evaluating machine translation: it measures n-gram overlap between the model's output and one or more human reference translations. ROUGE is designed for summarization (recall of overlapping units). Plain 'accuracy' does not capture translation quality. While BERTScore measures semantic similarity and is sometimes used, the metric specifically built for translation evaluation is BLEU.

question: A regional airline plans to launch a customer help assistant built with Amazon Bedrock to answer booking and policy questions. Which AWS feature will most improve the assistant’s factual accuracy and safe, consistent behavior?

options.B: Guardrails

question: ByteCraft Electronics operates a customer help portal where shoppers ask about device compatibility and warranty terms. The team needs Amazon Q Business to return precise answers that reflect catalog and policy changes that sync every 45 minutes from internal systems. Which capability of Amazon Q Business ensures answers stay accurate and relevant to the most current business data?

options.A: Generates responses only from the model’s general training data without checking company-specific sources
options.B: Uses Retrieval Augmented Generation to pull the latest catalog and policy content and ground the answer in that context
options.C: Delivers replies from a static, rule-driven knowledge base that is not updated in real time
options.D: Enforces guardrails and content filters to block unsafe topics instead of retrieving fresh business data

rationale:
The correct choice is Uses Retrieval Augmented Generation to pull the latest catalog and policy content and ground the answer in that context.
